metadata.title: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = tunneling current / quantum / works; CRIMSON = classical/gap too large/no current
metadata.note: Exclusions: no work-function derivation of kappa, no feedback-electronics detail, no barrier-matching algebra.

beats.narration_text: A scanning tunneling microscope can image individual atoms. Its secret is a number: moving the tip one atom's-width closer changes the current by a factor of ten.

card.copy: Why a Microscope Can Feel a Single Atom

beats.narration_text: The tip never touches. It hovers a few ångströms above the surface — closer than the width of a single atom — and electrons tunnel across the gap.
The current is flowing across a vacuum gap. Classically, electrons can't cross a gap wider than their energy allows. So why does any current flow at all?

card.copy:
The tip never touches.
Classically, no current should flow.
Why does it?

beats.narration_text: Inside the barrier, the wavefunction doesn't stop. It decays exponentially: psi falls as e to the minus kappa-d, where d is the gap width and kappa is about one per ångström for typical metal work functions.

The tunneling current is proportional to the squared amplitude: I proportional to e to the minus 2-kappa-d. Kappa is roughly 1.025 per ångström for a four electron-volt barrier — typical of most metals.

Add one extra ångström to the gap. The exponent grows by 2 kappa times one, which is about 2. So the current drops by e squared — a factor of roughly seven to ten. One ångström changes the current by an order of magnitude.

That extreme sensitivity is exactly what gives atomic resolution. The atom on the very tip of the probe is one ångström closer to the surface than the atoms one step back. Its current contribution is seven to ten times larger. The rest of the tip is nearly invisible.

As the tip scans sideways, each surface atom it passes over is the closest object for a moment. The current spikes, then falls. The current trace maps the atomic topography directly — peaks of current above atoms, valleys between them.

This is the same exponential that governs alpha decay in nuclei and electron transfer in photosynthesis. Quantum tunneling is not a curiosity. It is how biology extracts energy from sunlight, and how we image the atomic world.

The wavefunction decays in the gap as e to the minus kappa-d. Current falls as e to the minus 2-kappa-d. One ångström — one factor of ten. That exponential ruler gives the STM atomic resolution without ever touching.

card.copy:
psi ~ e^(-kd). Current ~ e^(-2kd).
1 angstrom = factor of ~7-10.
Exponential ruler: atomic resolution.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Foundations: The Quantum World series.
